skill.name: baoyu-post-to-x
skill.description: Post regular updates and long-form Markdown articles to X using Chrome browser automation, clipboard helpers, and optional submit mode.
skill.summary: Compose and publish X posts or X Articles from text, images, and Markdown through local Chrome automation.
skill.author: JimLiu

security_audit.summary: Static critical keylogger and weak-crypto findings are mostly false positives: the code sends paste keystrokes and uses MD5 only for cache filenames. However, the skill intentionally bypasses anti-automation checks, launches an authenticated Chrome profile under CDP control, and sends OS-level paste commands. No evidence found for credential exfiltration or prompt injection, but the confirmed browser-control behavior is high risk and should not be published without human approval.

high_findings.title: Intentional anti-automation evasion for X posting
high_findings.description: The skill states that it uses real Chrome to bypass anti-bot detection and launches Chrome with automation-hiding flags. This is not credential theft, but it is deliberate platform evasion using an authenticated browser session.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicitly documented and implemented with Chrome launch flags and CDP automation. Legitimate posting is possible, but the anti-detection intent is clear.

high_findings.title: OS-level input injection through paste automation
high_findings.description: The helper can activate applications and send real Cmd+V, Ctrl+V, or PowerShell SendKeys input. The scanner keylogger label is inaccurate because no keys are captured, but sending input to the active desktop is a high-risk capability.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts directly call OS automation tools to paste into the foreground application. This is confirmed input injection, though not keylogging.

high_findings.title: Authenticated Chrome control over a local DevTools endpoint
high_findings.description: The scripts open a Chrome debugging port, connect over WebSocket, and issue CDP commands against pages using a persistent profile. This expands local attack surface and controls logged-in browser state.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The CDP endpoint and WebSocket control are directly implemented. The code appears to target local Chrome, so this is high risk rather than confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: External command execution is broad but mostly argument-vector based
medium_findings.description: The skill invokes npx, bun, Chrome, osascript, xdotool, ydotool, Swift, and PowerShell helpers. Most calls use argument arrays instead of shell concatenation, lowering injection risk, but the capability remains sensitive.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and generally use fixed command names with argument arrays. Risk remains because several OS automation tools are launched.

medium_findings.title: Remote image downloads and HTML insertion lack strong validation
medium_findings.description: Markdown image URLs can be downloaded over HTTP or HTTPS into a temp directory, and Markdown is converted into HTML for browser insertion. The code does not enforce content length, content type, or full HTML sanitization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The downloader and HTML insertion paths are directly present. Impact depends on the Markdown source and X editor handling, so this is moderate rather than critical.

medium_findings.title: Persistent browser profile stores authenticated session state
medium_findings.description: The skill creates or reuses a persistent Chrome profile directory so X login cookies remain available between runs. No evidence found that it reads or exfiltrates cookies, but the stored session increases local sensitivity.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The profile behavior is documented and implemented. I did not find code that opens browser credential databases or sends cookies to a remote server.

low_findings.title: Scanner false positive: MD5 used as a cache filename key
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto hits map to MD5 usage for deterministic remote image cache names. This is not used for authentication, signing, or password storage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code hashes the image URL and truncates it to name a temporary file. There is no security decision based on this hash.

low_findings.title: Scanner false positive: documentation command examples
low_findings.description: Many Ruby backtick and external-command detections are Markdown examples showing how to run the provided scripts. These are usage instructions, not hidden execution paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are fenced Markdown command examples. They do not execute unless a user chooses to run them.

low_findings.title: Scanner false positive: no prompt injection or review override text found
low_findings.description: I did not find text that tries to override the audit process, claim pre-approval, or instruct the evaluator to skip security analysis. The evaluation file contains a prior audit opinion, not executable instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected skill files did not contain prompt-injection phrases. The cited evaluation summary is untrusted context and was not followed as an instruction.

dangerous_patterns.title: Chrome launched with automation-hiding flag
dangerous_patterns.description: Chrome is started with a flag that disables automation-controlled browser signals while navigating to X compose pages.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The launch arguments are directly visible and match the documented bypass behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Foreground application paste commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The paste helper sends real paste shortcuts through macOS, Linux, or Windows automation tools.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands and target keystrokes are explicit. They paste clipboard contents rather than record keyboard input.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local CDP WebSocket browser control
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts wait for Chrome DevTools metadata, connect to a WebSocket debugger URL, and send Runtime, DOM, and Input commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The CDP connection and command-sending abstraction are implemented in the scripts. It is local browser automation, but it controls authenticated page state.

content.user_title: Post to X From Claude, Codex, and Claude Code
content.value_statement: Publishing X posts and long-form articles from AI workflows can require repeated browser steps. This skill automates composition, image insertion, Markdown conversion, preview, and optional submission through local Chrome.
actual_capabilities: Compose regular X posts with text and up to four images.
Convert Markdown files into HTML for X Articles.
Insert article titles, cover images, body content, and inline images.
Copy images or HTML to the system clipboard across major desktop platforms.
Open Chrome with a persistent profile for saved X login sessions.
Preview by default and submit only when the submit flag is used.
limitations: Requires Chrome or Chromium, Bun, and local script execution.
Requires manual X login on first use and X Premium for Articles.
Uses desktop automation that may need accessibility permissions.
May break when X changes its interface or automation defenses.

use_cases.title: Publish creator updates
use_cases.description: Draft and preview text posts with screenshots before publishing to X.

use_cases.title: Turn Markdown into X Articles
use_cases.description: Convert a prepared article file into an X Article with a title, cover, and images.

use_cases.title: Share release notes
use_cases.description: Prepare product announcements with images from a local development workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Preview a text post
prompt_templates.prompt: Preview this X post without submitting: We shipped the new onboarding flow today.

prompt_templates.title: Add images to a post
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an X post with this text and attach ./screenshot.png. Keep it in preview mode.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare an article draft
prompt_templates.prompt: Open ./launch-notes.md as an X Article draft with ./cover.png as the cover image. Do not publish it.

prompt_templates.title: Publish after review
prompt_templates.prompt: Publish ./announcement.md to X Articles using the saved Chrome profile after I confirm the preview looks correct.

output_examples.input: Preview a post with one image.
output: Chrome opens to the X compose screen.
The text is inserted and the image is pasted from the clipboard.
The browser remains available for visual review before submission.

output_examples.input: Create an X Article from Markdown.
output: The Markdown title, body, links, and images are prepared for the article editor.
A cover image is uploaded when provided or inferred from the document.
The draft is left open so the user can inspect it before publishing.

best_practices: Use preview mode first and review the browser before submitting.
Use a dedicated Chrome profile for this skill instead of a primary browsing profile.
Verify every image path and remote image source before running article publishing.
anti_patterns: Do not run the submit option without inspecting the composed post.
Do not grant desktop automation permissions on shared or untrusted machines.
Do not use the skill to bypass platform rules, rate limits, or consent boundaries.

faq.question: Does this publish automatically?
faq.answer: Preview mode is the default. Publishing requires the submit option or an explicit user action.

faq.question: Why does it need Chrome?
faq.answer: The scripts control a real Chrome session through the Chrome DevTools Protocol.

faq.question: Does it store my X password?
faq.answer: No password storage was found. It reuses a Chrome profile where X login cookies may persist.

faq.question: Can it post X Articles?
faq.answer: Yes, but X Articles require an eligible X account and may require X Premium.

faq.question: What image formats are supported?
faq.answer: The clipboard helper supports JPEG, PNG, GIF, and WebP image files.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: It controls an authenticated browser, bypasses automation checks, and sends desktop paste commands.
